elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings indicate that at low temperatures, the photon ring radius decreases with increasing of chemical potential, while at high temperatures, the results are consistent with previous studies.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, we explore the impact of the model parameter $\lambda$ on the Einstein ring radius and find the the ring radius increases as the model parameter $\lambda$ increases.

elements.sent-2411-010: Conversely, for a small chemical potential ${u = 0.1$, the temperature dependence of the Einstein ring stays the same as $\lambda = 2$ and $\lambda = 4$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The General Theory of Relativity (GR), formulated in 1915, has been proved extensively to be validated on the Solar System and other astrophysical scales~\cite{Will2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: It forms the foundation of the Standard Cosmological Model.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, certain observations and theoretical issues, such as the non-renormalization of GR, the cosmological constant problem, the late acceleration of the Universe~\cite{SP1999 , and anomalous galaxy rotation curves~\cite{Sofue2001 , remain unresolved by GR alone.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In recent years, modified gravity theories have emerged as a broad class of models addressing these issues in the $\Lambda$CDM model and GR.
elements.sent-2411-015: These theories enhance cosmological evolution at both background and perturbation levels~\cite{Yashar2021 , including $f(R)$ gravity ~\cite{Cognola2008 , Gauss-Bonnet and $f(G)$ gravity~\cite{Antonio2009 , Lovelock gravity~\cite{Nathalie1990 , and others.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Among these, massive gravity ~\cite{Fierz1939 is notable for potentially explaining many cosmological problems without invoking dark energy.}
elements.sent-2411-017: There are many theories of massive gravity in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper we consider a massive gravity theory where the graviton acquires a mass due to the Lorentz symmetry breaking.
elements.sent-2411-019: Here Higgs mechanism for gravity is introduced with space-time depending scalar fields which are coupled to gravity.
elements.sent-2411-020: The resulting massive theory with Lorentz symmetry breaking exhibits modified gravitational interactions at large scale.
elements.sent-2411-021: And this theory is free from ghosts and tachyonic instabilities around the curved space as well as in the flat sapce.
elements.sent-2411-022: A more detailed review can be found in~\cite{Dubovsky:2004sg, Rubakov:2008nh .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This paper focus on the massive gravity with spontaneously broken Lorentz symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-024: Black shadows, representing the appearance of photons orbiting in unstable orbits around a black hole in an observer's sky~\cite{Ste2021, Ayz2018, Ku2022, An2023,Zeng:2020dco,Zeng:2020vsj , offer a way to test modified gravity theories.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The study of black holes and modern gravity has obtained interest not only from scientists but also from the general public, especially since the observation of Einstein rings~\cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2019dse, EventHorizonTelescope:2022wkp .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Specifically, an Einstein ring is formed by the deflection of photons emitted by light sources in the vicinity of a black hole~\cite{He2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: When light rays approach a black hole, photons with low orbital angular momentum are captured by the black hole, creating a dark zone in the sky that can be observed by distant observers.
elements.sent-2411-028: This phenomenon is well-known as the Einstein ring~\cite{Falcke:1999pj, Lu2014, Dexter2014, Broderick2014, Bromley2001, Noble2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Following the first observation of the Einstein ring, there has been a surge in theoretical modeling efforts~\cite{Bambi2019, Vagnozzi2019, Allahyari2020, Khodadi2020, Babar2020, Kumar2020 to study colossal galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In the papers by Hashimoto et al.~\cite{Hashimoto:2018okj, Hashimoto:2019jmw , a holographic method was proposed to study the Einstein ring using the AdS/CFT correspondence.}
elements.sent-2411-031: This holographic dual~\cite{Witten1998, Horatiu2015, AmmonandJ2015 relates a gravity theory, typically in a $(d+1)$-dimensional Anti-de Sitter (AdS) background, to a conformal field theory (CFT) in $d$ dimensions at the boundary of the space.}
elements.sent-2411-032: This has significantly advanced our understanding of string theory and quantum gravity.
elements.sent-2411-033: In~\cite{Hashimoto:2018okj, Hashimoto:2019jmw , Hashimoto and colleagues considered a quantum field theory (QFT) on $R_t \times S^2$ and introduced an external source on $S^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Their results demonstrated that the well-known Einstein rings could be reproduced using the holographic method.
elements.sent-2411-035: Building on this pioneering work, Liu et al.~\cite{Liu:2022cev investigated the holographic image of a charged AdS black hole using this approach.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Their results indicated that the radius of the ring in the image is affected by temperature but not by the chemical potential.
elements.sent-2411-037: Further studies on the holographic images of black holes have extended to various gravitational frameworks~\cite{Zeng:2023zlf, Zeng:2023tjb, Hu:2023eow, Zeng:2023ihy, Hu:2023mai,Zeng:2024ybj,Gui:2024owz .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Although the overarching concept is similar~\cite{Zeng:2023zlf, Zeng:2023tjb, Hu:2023eow, Zeng:2023ihy, Hu:2023mai,Zeng:2024ybj,Gui:2024owz , there are significant differences in the details.}
elements.sent-2411-039: For example, the photon sphere's characteristics vary according to the specific bulk dual geometry.
elements.sent-2411-040: In this paper, we investigate the behavior of the lensed response in a charged black hole within the context of Lorentz symmetry breaking massive gravity.
elements.sent-2411-041: First, we study the impact of the frequency $\omega$ on the Einstein ring, finding that higher frequencies have a diminishing effect on the ring.
elements.sent-2411-042: We then focus on the effect of the chemical potential $u$ on the Einstein ring's radius at both high and low temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our results show that at low temperatures, the Einstein ring radius decreases with increasing chemical potential, whereas at high temperatures, the results are consistent with the previous studies.
elements.sent-2411-044: We also examine the influence of the model parameter $\lambda$ on the Einstein ring radius for $\lambda = 2$ and $\lambda = 4$.
elements.sent-2411-045: When $\lambda = 2$, the charged black hole reverts to the Reissner-Nordström AdS (RN-AdS) black hole.
elements.sent-2411-046: For a chemical potential $u = 1$, the temperature dependence behavior of the photon ring radius is reversed between $\lambda = 2$ and $\lambda = 4$.
elements.sent-2411-047: For a small chemical potential, such as $u = 0.1$, the temperature dependence behavior of the Einstein ring is consistent for both $\lambda = 2$ and $\lambda = 4$.
elements.sent-2411-048: At present, there are indeed some works to study Einstein rings of black holes through holography.
elements.sent-2411-049: Especially in our previous work [33], we studied the effect of chemical potential on the ring where only the effect of chemical potential on the ring at high temperatures is considered.
elements.sent-2411-050: Here we further considered the case of low temperatures and found that the effect of chemical potential on the ring at low temperatures is completely different.
elements.sent-2411-051: On the other hand, we chose a Lorentz symmetry breaking massive gravity model, which can become to RN black holes under certain case.
elements.sent-2411-052: We also discuss the effects of temperature and chemical potential on the rings for different $\lambda$ at high temperature and low temperature with large chemical potential and small chemical potential.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this paper, the influence of chemical potential and temperature on the Einstein rings are more comprehensively and profoundly.
elements.sent-2411-054: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-055: In Sec.~\ref{Introduction , we introduce the charged black hole solution in Lorentz symmetry breaking massive gravity.}
elements.sent-2411-056: In Sec.~\ref{construction , we construct the holographic images and analyze the effects of the frequency $\omega$ and the chemical potential $u$ on the Einstein rings.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In Sec.~\ref{3 , we compare the results obtained from the holographic method with those derived from geometric optics, demonstrating that the position of the photon ring obtained from geometric optics is in full agreement with that of the holographic ring.}
elements.section-2411-002: The charged black hole solution in Lorentz symmetry breaking in massive gravity
elements.sent-2411-058: As we know, in 1939, massive gravity was first proposed by Fierz and Pauli~\cite{Fierz1939 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Here, we consider the Lorentz symmetry breaking massive gravity~\cite{Dubovsky:2004sg, Rubakov:2008nh .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The action of this Lorentz symmetry breaking massive gravity is given by \begin{equation}S=\int d^4 x \sqrt{-g} \left[ -M_{pl}^2 \mathcal{R}+\Omega^4 \mathcal{F}(X,W^{ij}) \right],\end{equation} here $\mathcal{R}$ is the scalar curvature and the function $\mathcal{F}$ describes the scalar fields $\Xi^0$ and $\Xi^{i}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The scalar field is minimally coupled to gravity while in the theory the Lorentz symmetry is spontaneously broken.
elements.sent-2411-062: It is possible to express the function $\mathcal{F}$ in terms of two Goldstone fields \(X\) and \(W^{ij}\).
elements.sent-2411-063: The expressions for \(X\) and \(W^{ij}\) are given as~\cite{Liang:2017ceh, Fernando:2018fpq, Fernando:2016qhq, Liu:2017jbm \begin{eqnarray}X=\frac{\partial^{\rho}\Xi^0 \partial_{\rho}\Xi^{0}}{\Omega^4},\end{eqnarray} and \begin{equation}W^{ij}=\frac{\partial^{\rho}\Xi^i \partial_{\rho}\Xi^{j}}{\Omega^4}-\frac{\partial^{\rho}\Xi^i \partial_{\rho}\Xi^{0}\partial^{\sigma}\Xi^{j}\partial_{\sigma}\Xi^0}{\Omega^4}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Finding analytical solutions for a generic function $\mathcal{F}$ is not possible.
elements.sent-2411-065: We can choose this function in such a way that the resulting equations are solvable analytically.
elements.sent-2411-066: We consider a static and spherically symmetric metric~\cite{Liang:2017ceh, Fernando:2018fpq, Fernando:2016qhq, Liu:2017jbm \begin{equation}ds^2=-G(r)dt^2+K(r)dr^2+r^2 (d\psi^2+\sin^2 \psi d\xi^2)\end{equation} with \begin{eqnarray}\Xi^{0}=\Omega^2(t+N(r)), \ \ \ \Xi^{i}=\xi(r)\frac{\Omega^2 x^i}{r},\end{eqnarray} along with the choice~\cite{Liang:2017ceh, Fernando:2018fpq, Fernando:2016qhq, Liu:2017jbm \begin{equation}\mathcal{F}=c_0 (\frac{1}{X}+w_{1})+c_1(w_1^3-2w_1w_2-6w_1+2w_3-12),\end{equation} where $c_0$ and $c_1$ are some dimensionless constants and \begin{eqnarray}w_1=-(f_1+2f_2), w_2=f_1^2+2f_2^2,w_3=-(f_1^3+2f_2^3),\end{eqnarray} with \begin{eqnarray}f_1=\frac{\xi^{\prime 2}}{GKX^{\prime}}, \ f_2=\frac{\xi^2}{r^2},\ X=\frac{K-G N^{\prime 2}}{GK}.\end{eqnarray}.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: With the above \(\mathcal{F}\), a static spherically symmetric metric solution of a charged AdS black hole is as follows~\cite{Bebronne:2009mz \begin{equation}ds^2=-G(r)dt^2+\frac{1}{G(r)}dr^2+r^2(d\psi^2+\sin^2\psi d\xi^2), \label{metric}\end{equation} and the corresponding metric function is \begin{equation}G(r)=1-\frac{2M}{r}-\chi\frac{Q^2}{r^{\lambda}}-\frac{\Lambda r^2}{3}, \label{G}\end{equation} where \(\Lambda\) is the cosmological constant.}
elements.sent-2411-068: For more details, please refer to the ~\cite{Liang:2017ceh, Fernando:2018fpq, Fernando:2016qhq, Liu:2017jbm .}
elements.sent-2411-069: At the event horizon, the Hawking temperature is calculated as follows \begin{equation}T=\frac{f^{\prime}}{4\pi}=\frac{1}{4\pi} \left( \frac{1}{r_h}-r_h\Lambda+\frac{\chi (\lambda-1)}{r_h^{\lambda+1}}Q^2 \right).\label{tr}\end{equation} Here, we carefully analyze the two parameters $\chi$ and $\lambda$ in Eq. (\ref{G ).}
elements.sent-2411-070: For $\chi=-1$, this metric solution is similar to the well-known Reissner-Nordström AdS black hole, which admits a nontrivial small or large black hole phase transition~\cite{Fernando:2016qhq .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Therefore, we choose $\chi=-1$ in this paper in order to compare our results with the case of Reissner-Nordström AdS black hole.
elements.sent-2411-072: For the parameter $\lambda$, the ADM mass will diverge if $\lambda<1$, which is not allowed~\cite{Fernando:2016sps , so we let $\lambda>1$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: To sum up, in this paper we consider the case for $\chi=-1$ and $\lambda>1$.
elements.sent-2411-074: Next, we focus on the spherical metric (\ref{G ).}
elements.sent-2411-075: With a new definition \(r_*=1/r\), we have \(F(r)=r_*^{-2 F(r_*)\).}
elements.sent-2411-076: We rewrite the metric (\ref{G ) in the new coordinates \((t, r_*, \theta, \phi)\) and derive \begin{equation}ds^2=\frac{1}{r_*^2}\left[-F(r_*)dt^2+\frac{d r_*^2}{F(r_*)}+d\Omega^2\right], \label{metric_2}\end{equation} where \(d\Omega^2=d\theta^2+\sin^2\theta d\phi^2\).}
elements.sent-2411-077: We will take Eq. (\ref{metric_2 ) as our background next.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The complex scalar field \(\Phi\) in such a background is determined by the Klein-Gordon equation~\cite{Liu:2022cev \begin{equation}D_b D^b \Phi - M^2\Phi=0,\end{equation} where \(D_a=\nabla_a - i e A_a\) and we will take \(M^2=-2\) in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-079: It is worth pointing out that we consider the weak coupling problem and only the effect of the metric on the scalar field is involved, meaning the back reaction of the scalar field on the metric is not included.
elements.sent-2411-080: To solve the above Klein-Gordon equation easily, the ingoing Eddington coordinate is introduced, which is expressed as \begin{equation}\nu=t-\int \frac{d r_*}{F(r_*)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-081: With the above new coordinate, the metric (\ref{metric_2 ) is further expressed as \begin{equation}ds^2=\frac{1}{r_*^2}\left[-F(r_*)d\nu^2-2 d r_* d\nu+d\Omega^2\right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The corresponding asymptotic solution of this complex scalar field \(\Phi\) near the AdS boundary is~\cite{Liu:2022cev \begin{equation}\Phi(\nu,r_*,\theta,\phi)=r_* J_{\mathcal{O}}(\nu,\theta,\phi)+r_*^2 \langle \mathcal O \rangle + \mathrm{O}(r_*^3).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Based on the AdS/CFT correspondence, \(J_{\mathcal{O }\) is the source for the boundary field theory, and its expectation value is \begin{equation}\langle \mathcal O \rangle_{J_{\mathcal{O}}} = \langle \mathcal O \rangle - (\partial_{\nu} - ie u) J_{\mathcal{O}},\end{equation} where \(\langle \mathcal{O \rangle_{J_{\mathcal{O }\) is also called the response function, and \(u= Q/r_h\) is the chemical potential.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: It is evident that \(\langle \mathcal{O \rangle\) corresponds to the expectation value of the dual operator when the source is turned off.}
elements.section-2411-003: Holographic images construction of the charged black hole in Lorentz symmetry breaking massive gravity
elements.sent-2411-085: In 2019, the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) collaboration claimed the first capture of a shadow image of the supermassive black hole at the center of the galaxy M87~\cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2022wkp .}
elements.sent-2411-086: This breakthrough has sparked increased scientific interest in black hole shadow research.
elements.sent-2411-087: In this section, we introduce the holographic construction of Einstein rings for charged black holes in Lorentz symmetry-breaking massive gravity.
elements.sent-2411-088: Similar to previous holographic studies, the external source is mapped to the boundary condition of the bulk field.
elements.sent-2411-089: A probe wave emitted from the source located on the AdS boundary is diffracted by the black hole as it traverses through the black hole spacetime, ultimately reaching the opposite side of the AdS boundary, as depicted in Fig.~\ref{go_es .}
elements.figure-2411-001: [H] \includegraphics[scale=0.55]{figure1.pdf} \caption{The ingoing angle and outgoing angle corresponding to the moving of the photons around the black hole. } \label{go_es}

elements.sent-2411-092: When $\sigma \ll 1$, the Gaussian tail is negligible, and the Gaussian function decomposes into scalar spherical harmonics.

elements.sent-2411-227: The Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) achieved a groundbreaking milestone in 2019 by capturing the first image of the shadow of the Supermassive Black Hole (SMBH) in the center of the $M87^{*}$ galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-228: This discovery opens up new avenues for constraining modified theories of gravity, as the shadow's size can be influenced by both new parameters introduced by alternative gravity models and the specific properties of the black hole solution, which differ from those predicted by General Relativity (GR).
elements.sent-2411-229: In this study, we focus on investigating the Einstein ring of a charged black hole within Lorentz symmetry breaking massive gravity using the holographic method.
elements.sent-2411-230: We analyze phenomena such as photon rings, luminosity-deformed rings, or light points at various observation positions.
elements.sent-2411-231: Initially, we explored the influence of the parameter $\lambda$ in our gravity model on the response function, finding that different values of $\lambda$ exhibit nearly identical effects on the correlation function.
elements.sent-2411-232: Additionally, we investigated the impact of the frequency $\bar{\omega}$ on the Einstein ring, noting that higher frequencies tend to align more closely with geometric optics principles, thereby exerting less influence on the Einstein ring.
elements.sent-2411-233: A critical aspect of our investigation focuses on the role of the chemical potential $u$ on the Einstein ring.
elements.sent-2411-234: Unlike previous studies that predominantly examined the effect of chemical potential on the ring radius at high temperatures, we extended our analysis to include low temperature scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-235: Our findings reveal that at low temperatures (as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig18 ) and high temperatures (as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig20 ), the radius of the photon ring decreases with increasing chemical potential $u$, consistent with prior research.}}
elements.sent-2411-236: Furthermore, we examined how the model parameter $\lambda$ impacts the radius of the Einstein ring.
elements.sent-2411-237: Specifically, for $\lambda=2$, the black hole characteristics resemble those of the RN-AdS black hole.
elements.sent-2411-238: Interestingly, for a chemical potential $u=1$, the relationship between the radius of the photon ring and temperature differs between $\lambda=2$ and $\lambda=4$.
elements.sent-2411-239: Conversely, for a smaller chemical potential such as $u=0.1$, the dependence of the Einstein ring on temperature remains consistent between $\lambda=2$ and $\lambda=4$.
elements.sent-2411-240: The difference is that through the holographic scheme, we find that the shadow shape is related to the wave source, such as the wave source frequency.
elements.sent-2411-241: Of course, the shadow image of any black hole can be compared with the results obtained by the EHT, and the parameters of the gravitational model can be restricted.
elements.sent-2411-242: And there is a lot of work in this direction.
elements.sent-2411-243: This paper does not focus on this problem, so it does not restrict the parameters of the black holes based on the EHT data, but in principle, it is feasible.
elements.sent-2411-244: In conclusion, our study underscores the pivotal role of holographic images in discerning the geometric nuances of different black holes within a fixed wave source and optical system setup.
elements.sent-2411-245: This research contributes to our understanding of how varying parameters in modified gravity models can alter observable phenomena such as the Einstein ring, thereby enriching our grasp of gravitational physics beyond the predictions of General Relativity.
